annotations.label: Roman numerals (definition)

anchor.snippet: Roman numerals are a numeral system that originated in ancient Rome

annotations.note: Grep for "roman" in Mathlib/ returns no files; Roman numeral system is not formalized.

annotations.label: Additive principle

anchor.snippet: Roman numerals are based on the additive principle

annotations.note: No Roman numeral encoding exists in Mathlib, so its additive principle is unformalized.

annotations.label: Seven symbol letters

anchor.snippet: uses only seven letters from the Latin alphabet

annotations.note: The seven Roman symbol letters (I, V, X, L, C, D, M) are not represented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: XXVII = 27

anchor.snippet: the Roman numeral XXVII represents the number

annotations.note: No Roman numeral parsing exists in Mathlib to give XXVII = 27.

annotations.label: Subtractive principle

anchor.snippet: When a smaller numeral symbol precedes a larger one, subtraction is implied

annotations.note: The subtractive notation rule for Roman numerals is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: MCM = 1900 / MCMXII = 1912

anchor.snippet: so 1912 is written MCMXII

annotations.note: No Roman numeral evaluation supporting MCMXII = 1912 is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: MMXXVI = 2026

anchor.snippet: is written MMXXVI

annotations.note: Mathlib contains no Roman numeral encoding to express MMXXVI = 2026.

annotations.label: Symbol per power of ten, no zero

anchor.snippet: use different symbols for each power of ten

annotations.note: The Roman per-power-of-ten symbol scheme is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Standard modern form

anchor.snippet: how Roman numerals are usually written in modern times

annotations.note: There is no definition of standard-form Roman numerals in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Subtractive notation for 4 and 9

anchor.snippet: are written using subtractive notation

annotations.note: The IV/IX subtractive convention is not defined anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Building multi-digit numbers

anchor.snippet: is built by appending the Roman numeral equivalent for each

annotations.note: No construction of Roman numerals by appending per-place symbols exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Missing place omitted

anchor.snippet: is omitted, as in Latin

annotations.note: The rule that empty decimal places are omitted in Roman numerals is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Largest representable number is 3,999

anchor.snippet: The largest number that can be represented in this manner

annotations.note: No theorem bounding standard Roman numerals at 3999 exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Year numbers (1776, etc.)

anchor.snippet: the date written on the book held by the Statue of Liberty

annotations.note: Roman numeral year examples are not represented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Additive forms IIII, XXXX, CCCC

anchor.snippet: additive notation to represent these numbers

annotations.note: Alternative additive Roman forms like IIII are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Colosseum gates use IIII

anchor.snippet: IIII is systematically used instead of IV

annotations.note: This historical Roman numeral usage is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 5 and 50 as IIIII, XXXXX

anchor.snippet: have been occasionally written IIIII and XXXXX

annotations.note: These additive Roman variants are not represented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Clock faces use IIII for four

anchor.snippet: still very often use IIII for four o'clock

annotations.note: Clock-face Roman numeral conventions are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Variant forms for 1900

anchor.snippet: use variant forms for

annotations.note: Variant Roman forms for 1900 are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: IIX for 8

anchor.snippet: historical examples of IIX being used for 8

annotations.note: The historical IIX = 8 form is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Other subtractive forms (IIIXX, IIC, IC)

anchor.snippet: historical examples of other subtractive forms

annotations.note: Nonstandard subtractive Roman forms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Excel ROMAN() subtraction modes

anchor.snippet: supports multiple subtraction modes

annotations.note: Spreadsheet ROMAN() conversion behavior is unrelated to anything in Mathlib.

annotations.label: IIXX for 22 (XXII Legion)

anchor.snippet: IIXX was how people associated with the XXII Roman Legion

annotations.note: This nonstandard Roman numeral variant is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: VXL for 45

anchor.snippet: VXL for 45, instead of the usual XLV

annotations.note: The VXL = 45 variant is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: No numeral for zero

anchor.snippet: lacked a numeral for the number zero itself

annotations.note: The absence of a Roman zero symbol is not a formalized notion in Mathlib.

annotations.label: nulla used for 0

anchor.snippet: was used to represent 0

annotations.note: The medieval "nulla" zero word is not represented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Letter N for 0

anchor.snippet: used the letter N

annotations.note: Using N for zero in Roman numerals is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Arabic 0 to open enumerations

anchor.snippet: has been used as a zero to open enumerations

annotations.note: This typographic enumeration convention is unrelated to anything in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Roman fraction S = 1/2

anchor.snippet: The base

annotations.note: Mathlib has the number 1/2 but no Roman fractional symbol S is defined.

annotations.label: S equals 6/12 not 5/10

anchor.snippet: as one might expect

annotations.note: The Roman duodecimal interpretation of S is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Duodecimal fraction system

anchor.snippet: used a duodecimal rather than a decimal system for fractions

annotations.note: The Roman duodecimal fraction system is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dot per uncia

anchor.snippet: are indicated by a dot

annotations.note: The dot-per-uncia fraction notation is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Named twelfth-fractions

anchor.snippet: had a name in Roman times

annotations.note: Named Roman twelfth-fractions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Slash through last letter

anchor.snippet: with a slash through the last letter in a numeral

annotations.note: This Roman fractional notation device is not represented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Modern form limited to 3999

anchor.snippet: can only write numbers up to 3999

annotations.note: No formalization of the 3999 limit on standard Roman numerals exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Apostrophus notation

anchor.snippet: Using the apostrophus method

annotations.note: The apostrophus large-number notation is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Apostrophus factor-of-ten rule

anchor.snippet: raises the value by a factor of ten

annotations.note: The apostrophus factor-of-ten rule is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Apostrophus reductions to D, ↀ, etc.

anchor.snippet: is reduced to

annotations.note: Apostrophus glyph reductions are not represented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vinculum multiplies by 1,000

anchor.snippet: conventional Roman numerals are multiplied by 1,000

annotations.note: The vinculum ×1000 convention is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Three-sided box multiplies by 100,000

anchor.snippet: is used to multiply by 100,000

annotations.note: The three-sided-box ×100000 convention is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Year as two numerals (XVIXIII)

anchor.snippet: written as two Roman numerals

annotations.note: Splitting years into two Roman numerals is not represented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: IIII XX XIX for 99

anchor.snippet: constructions like IIII XX XIX for 99

annotations.note: The vigesimal-style Roman construction for 99 is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 13,573 in medieval accounting

anchor.snippet: renders numbers like 13,573

annotations.note: Medieval accounting Roman numeral rendering is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Derived from Etruscan numerals

anchor.snippet: are directly derived from the Etruscan number symbols

annotations.note: The Etruscan origin of Roman numerals is a historical claim not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Etruscan 87

anchor.snippet: the number 87, for example

annotations.note: Etruscan numeral examples are not represented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kayser halving derivation

anchor.snippet: the intermediate ones were derived by taking half of those

annotations.note: Kayser's halving derivation of Roman symbols is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Colosseum gate 44 = XLIIII

anchor.snippet: gate 44 is labelled XLIIII

annotations.note: This Colosseum gate Roman numeral example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lowercase Roman numerals

anchor.section: Use in the Middle Ages and Renaissance
anchor.snippet: lower-case versions of Roman numbers

annotations.note: Lowercase Roman numeral forms are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chronograms

anchor.section: Use in the Middle Ages and Renaissance
anchor.snippet: messages with dates encoded into them

annotations.note: Chronograms encoding dates in Roman numerals are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Medieval Roman numerals

anchor.snippet: sometimes include additional symbols

annotations.note: Extended medieval Roman numeral symbols are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quadrants I–IV in mathematics

anchor.snippet: its quadrants are named using

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named Cartesian-plane quadrants labeled with Roman numerals.

annotations.label: Examples 39, 246, 789, 2421

anchor.snippet: 39 = XXX + IX = XXXIX

annotations.note: Explicit multi-digit Roman numeral construction examples have no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Apostrophus IↃ = 500, CIↃ = 1,000

anchor.snippet: 500 is written as IↃ , while 1,000 is written as CIↃ

annotations.note: The specific apostrophus encodings of 500 and 1000 are not represented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vinculum IV = 4,000, XXV = 25,000
annotations.note: Specific vinculum-multiplied Roman numerals are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-positional numeral system

anchor.snippet: As a non- positional numeral system

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define a general notion of positional vs. non-positional numeral systems for Roman numerals.
